title: Rootless Podman POD leaks memory when restarting containers inside the pod (pasta backend, memory.current grows permanently even after pod restart)
body:
### Issue Description

Rootless Podman (tested on Podman 5.7.1) exhibits a persistent memory leak when:

* a POD has multiple containers
* containers are restarted individually via systemd --user
* networking backend is pasta (default in Podman 5.x rootless mode)
* images are updated by retagging to :current (causing new overlayfs layers)

After these restarts, memory in the rootless cgroup:
`/sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-UID.slice/user@UID.service/memory.current`

increases permanently by hundreds of MB to >1 GB, and does not return to baseline even after:
`systemctl --user restart <POD>-pod.service`

The leak is not in the restarted containers themselves (RSS remains small), but memory remains pinned in the rootless user.slice, suggesting leaked:

* mount namespaces
* pasta network resources
* overlayfs layer references
* infra-container namespace state

This behavior is fully reproducible with a minimal Quadlet-based POD (no SELinux tweaks, no external workloads).

### Steps to reproduce the issue

# Initialization
Boot a Fedora Core OS image and login.

## Create the Quadlet POD file
Write to `~/.config/containers/systemd/testpod.pod`
```
[Pod]
PodName=testpod
```

## Create the first container (big image + healthcheck)
Use Ubuntu (larger rootfs = easier leak).
Write to `~/.config/containers/systemd/web.container:`

```
[Unit]
Description=web test
After=testpod-pod.service
Requires=testpod-pod.service

[Container]
# IMPORTANT: use :current tag to simulate updates
Image=ubuntu:current
ContainerName=web
Pod=testpod.pod
SecurityLabelDisable=true

# Simulate a “service” that opens port 80 slowly
Exec=/bin/sh -c "sleep 15 && python3 -m http.server 80"

# Add healthcheck (forces systemd restarts while web is starting/stopping)
HealthCmd=/bin/sh -c "echo > /dev/tcp/127.0.0.1/80"
HealthInterval=5s
HealthTimeout=2s
HealthRetries=3
HealthStartPeriod=30s

[Service]
Restart=always
RestartSec=1
```

## Create the second container (sleeper)
Write to `~/.config/containers/systemd/sleeper.container:`
```
[Unit]
Description=sleeper test
After=testpod-pod.service
Requires=testpod-pod.service

[Container]
Image=busybox:current
ContainerName=sleeper
Pod=testpod.pod
SecurityLabelDisable=true
Exec=sleep 999999

[Service]
Restart=always
RestartSec=1
```

## Load the initial images and tag them to :current
```
podman pull ubuntu:22.04
podman tag ubuntu:22.04 ubuntu:current

podman pull busybox:1.36
podman tag busybox:1.36 busybox:current
```

## Start the systemd units
```
systemctl --user daemon-reload
systemctl --user start web.service sleeper.service
```

## Verify pod and containers:
```
podman pod ps
podman ps
```

## Get baseline memory
`cat /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-$(id -u).slice/user@$(id -u).service/memory.current`

# Trigger the leak with update cycles

## Cycle A
```
podman pull ubuntu:22.10
podman tag ubuntu:22.10 ubuntu:current

systemctl --user restart web.service
sleep 5
```

## Cycle B
```
podman pull ubuntu:23.04
podman tag ubuntu:23.04 ubuntu:current

systemctl --user restart sleeper.service
sleep 5
```

## Cycle C
```
podman pull ubuntu:23.10
podman tag ubuntu:23.10 ubuntu:current

systemctl --user restart web.service
sleep 5
```

## Repeat cycle A and B again
Repeat cycle A and B again, this time no images need to pulled

## Check memory again
`cat /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-$(id -u).slice/user@$(id -u).service/memory.current`

## Check namepsaces
`lsns -t mnt | grep $(id -u)`

# POD restart
## Restart the whole pod
`systemctl --user restart testpod-pod.service`

## Check memory
`cat /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-$(id -u).slice/user@$(id -u).service/memory.current`


### Describe the results you received

Observe that memory usage went from 161005568 (161MB) to 1419702272 (1.4GB).
Also a pod restart did not clear the memory.

Output from my run:
```
Fedora CoreOS 43.20260217.3.1
```

### Describe the results you expected

I expected that restarting a single container inside a rootless Podman POD would:

* reuse the existing infra container safely
* release any old container‑specific pasta or mount‑namespace resources
* not create additional persistent namespace or overlayfs references
* keep memory usage stable inside:
`/sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-UID.slice/user@UID.service/memory.current`


and specifically:
Memory should remain close to the baseline value
(e.g. ~161 MB in my test case)
even after:

* retagging images to :current
* pulling new image layers
* restarting web.service and sleeper.service individually
* healthchecks failing and triggering systemd restarts

Additionally:
Restarting the entire POD
via:
`systemctl --user restart testpod-pod.service`

should:

* fully tear down and recreate the infra container
* drop all mount namespaces
* clean all pasta networking state
* release all overlayfs layer references
* return memory to the baseline level

In other words:

Container‑level restarts inside a rootless POD should not cause permanent memory growth, and a pod‑level restart should always fully clean all leaked resources.

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Running on a Mac M2, running Fedora core os via Qemu.

No sure if it is relevant, but the ulimits are raised in the ingition file with:
```
csst soft nofile 65536
csst hard nofile 65536
root soft nofile 65536
root hard nofile 65536
```

And network settings are:
```
# Faster cleanup of half-closed sockets
net.ipv4.tcp_fin_timeout = 10
net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse = 1

# Higher port range to reduce exhaustion risk
net.ipv4.ip_local_port_range = 1024 65000

# Stronger accept queue for reconnect storms
net.core.somaxconn = 4096
net.core.netdev_max_backlog = 16384
net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog = 8192

# Better keepalives for scanners that roam or sleep
net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_time = 300
net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_intvl = 30
net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_probes = 5
```

### Additional information

I also saw the same behavior on Fedora Live CD installed on a Intel N100 mini-pc running on bare-metal hardware when using [Kube] with 2 containers, It shows the same memory leak.

I do not have repro scenario for this, since I needed to switch to pod + containers mechanism.

comments.body:
**DIsclaimer**: I have AI help me to write this as best as possible, so sorry if it is a bit AIish.

# Additional Findings: Restarting podman.service is not enough — leaks persist until full user session termination
I continued investigating the memory leak behavior, and I found something important that seems relevant to rootless Podman internals.
Summary: restarting podman.service does not free the leaked memory.
A full rootless user session reset does.

# Restarting podman.service does NOT reclaim the leaked memory
During updates I tried:
```
systemctl --user restart podman.service
sleep 2
systemctl --user restart my-pod.service
```
Even after restarting Podman completely, memory in:
`/sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/memory.current`
continued to grow after each update involving **new** images (for my situation):
`1095 MB → 2093 MB → 2963 MB`

So podman.service restart does not clean everything.

# The leak lives in the user@1000.service slice, not under podman.service
All leaked memory is accounted under:
/sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service

This strongly suggests:

* leaked mount namespaces
* leaked overlayfs lowerdir references
* leaked pasta/slirp memory
* leaked kernel objects
* leaked netns mappings

are pinned in the _rootless user session_, not in Podman’s own cgroup.
Restarting Podman cannot reclaim memory that lives outside its cgroup.

# Only full user session termination fully reclaims memory
When I run:
`loginctl terminate-user csst`
After the session restarts (lingering user enabled), everything comes back clean.
This is the only operation (besides reboot) that fully drops the leaked memory.

# Conclusion
This suggests that rootless Podman triggers creation of kernel-level resources inside the user namespace that outlive Podman's lifecycle.
These resources accumulate across updates and are never freed until the user session exits.
This appears consistent with:

* leaked mount namespaces
* leaked overlayfs references
* leaked passt/slirp4netns buffers
* leaked infra netns links

but outside Podman’s own cgroup.
Restarting Podman does not free them, because they are pinned by the user session itself.
A full user session teardown does.

Okay, I investigated more and will close the PR. It is not a memory leak, but a misunderstanding my side in how to read 
system memory. I was checking the machine metrics for `system.mem.free` but this includes the cache/buff and should have monitored `system.mem.usable`. This was after I filed this PR.

So I reran the scenario again, but now with memory.stat instead of only memory.current. And now I learned that memory.current ≈ anon + file + kernel.  Because memory.current includes file, every time you pull a new 72MB Ubuntu image, memory.current jumped by ~72MB. It looked like Podman was eating RAM, but it was actually just the kernel keeping a copy of the image files in the spare RAM "cushion."

Baseline:
```
[csst@99192-CS01 ~]$ cat /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-$(id -u).slice/user@$(id -u).service/memory.current
161710080
[csst@99192-CS01 ~]$ cat /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-$(id -u).slice/user@$(id -u).service/memory.stat
anon 22429696
file 128446464
kernel 10563584
```

after the cycles:
```
[csst@99192-CS01 ~]$ cat /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-$(id -u).slice/user@$(id -u).service/memory.current
469172224
[csst@99192-CS01 ~]$ cat /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-$(id -u).slice/user@$(id -u).service/memory.stat
anon 22478848
file 420102144
kernel 26337280
```


And after asking the kernel to drop caches:
```
[csst@99192-CS01 ~]$ sync
[csst@99192-CS01 ~]$ echo 3 | sudo tee /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches
3
[csst@99192-CS01 ~]$ cat /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-$(id -u).slice/user@$(id -u).service/memory.current
81862656
[csst@99192-CS01 ~]$ cat /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-$(id -u).slice/user@$(id -u).service/memory.stat
anon 22474752
file 54665216
kernel 4419584
```

I am really sorry for the confusion.
